question: 'Overall Equipment Effectiveness' (OEE) is a key manufacturing performance metric calculated as:
options: Total machine hours divided by total production output units

Availability × Performance × Quality: Availability = (Planned Production Time - Downtime) / Planned Production Time; measures whether the equipment is running when it should be; Performance = (Actual Output Rate / Maximum Theoretical Output Rate); measures whether the equipment runs at full speed; Quality = (Good Units / Total Units Produced); measures the percentage of output that meets quality standards; example: a machine runs 8 hours/day, experiences 30 minutes downtime (Availability = 7.5/8 = 93.75%), runs at 80% of rated speed (Performance = 80%), and produces 98% good units (Quality = 98%); OEE = 93.75% × 80% × 98% = 73.5%; world-class OEE is considered 85%+; most manufacturers are 40-60%; the metric reveals where to focus improvement: low availability → fix downtime causes; low performance → fix speed losses (minor stoppages, reduced speed); low quality → fix defect causes; the combined metric: a machine that seems fine on any single dimension may still be performing at 60% OEE when all three are combined
OEE measures the number of defect-free units produced in a single shift
OEE is only applicable to semiconductor manufacturing processes
explanation: OEE as a lean manufacturing foundation: OEE was developed as part of Toyota's Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) framework. The three losses it measures are called the 'Six Big Losses': (1) Breakdown losses (unplanned downtime); (2) Setup and adjustment losses (planned downtime); (3) Minor stoppages; (4) Reduced speed; (5) Startup defects; (6) Production defects. Improving OEE from 60% to 80% on a $10M machine effectively creates $2M in additional capacity without capital investment — one reason why TPM investment has high ROI. The hospital equipment parallel: medical imaging equipment (MRI, CT) can be tracked with OEE logic: a scanner operating at 80% OEE (vs. 60% current) could scan 33% more patients without buying new equipment. Healthcare has been slower to adopt OEE but increasingly applies it to high-cost equipment.

question: A delivery company measures 'On-Time Delivery' (OTD) at 94% — 94% of packages delivered by the promised date. The operations team says this is excellent. Customer service reports it's inadequate. Which view is more likely correct and why?
options: 94% OTD is always excellent performance for any delivery company

Customer experience impact makes 94% OTD a problematic metric despite sounding high: the math of 94% OTD at scale: if the company delivers 500,000 packages/month, 6% failure = 30,000 failed deliveries/month; each failed delivery generates: customer complaint (cost), redelivery cost, potential return of goods, customer churn risk; 30,000 unhappy customers/month is significant operational and reputational impact. The asymmetry of quality perception: research consistently shows that a single service failure has 3-10× the negative impact on customer perception as a success of equal magnitude; customers who receive packages on time don't thank the delivery company; customers whose packages are late call, complain, and sometimes post negatively on social media; the '94% OTD' story doesn't reveal: which segments fail (are the 6% failures concentrated in certain routes, weather conditions, or customer types?); what the distribution of lateness is (1 day late vs. 5 days late have very different customer impact); how the 6% failure compares to competitors (if competitors average 97%, 94% is poor). The Amazon benchmark: Amazon Prime promises 1-2 day delivery and achieves ~98% OTD; customers who experience the 2% failure typically receive credits; 2% failure at 10M daily shipments = 200,000 customer contacts/day — still a major operational challenge at scale
6% failure rates are always acceptable in logistics businesses
Customer satisfaction metrics should always override operational efficiency metrics
explanation: The 99% quality problem: a classic total quality management teaching — any process that has 99% perfection sounds excellent until you calculate scale. 99% on-time flights would mean 8 crashes per day in the US; 99.9% purity in pharmaceutical manufacturing means contaminants that could harm patients; 99% uptime for a web service means 8.7 hours of downtime per year. The sigma level standard in quality: Six Sigma (6σ) = 3.4 defects per million opportunities. This is the standard for critical processes (semiconductor manufacturing, aircraft safety). 94% OTD = ~60,000 DPMO (defects per million opportunities) — a 3-sigma level, which is average industrial quality, not excellent. The Net Promoter Score (NPS) implications: every failed delivery generates a potential Detractor (NPS 0-6); the delivery company with 94% OTD is generating 30,000 Detractors/month — each representing a customer who may cancel, complain publicly, or recommend against the service.

question: Amazon's fulfillment centers track 'units picked per hour' (UPH) for warehouse workers. Workers know their UPH is tracked and managed against targets. Workers report: the metric drives pace, but has led to injury rates higher than industry average, and creates pressure to skip legally-required breaks. What does this case reveal about operations metrics and their unintended consequences?
options: Amazon should eliminate all performance metrics to reduce employee injury risk

Operations metrics that incentivize quantity without quality or safety create systematic problems: the metric design flaw: UPH measures output but not: safety (injury rate), quality (picking accuracy), sustainability (can the worker sustain this pace over a shift or a career?), or break compliance; when workers are incentivized purely on speed, they: skip breaks (reduces overall UPH since breaks pause the counter); move unsafely (faster movement = higher injury risk); pick faster at the expense of accuracy. The documented Amazon outcome: Amazon warehouse injury rates are approximately 2× the industry average; OSHA has cited Amazon facilities multiple times; the Senate investigation revealed that Amazon's time-off-task monitoring (penalizing workers for excessive 'idle time') contributed to workers avoiding bathroom breaks. The metric redesign options: (1) Composite metric: weight UPH with quality factor (picking error rate reduces score), safety factor (zero weight for time spent on safety breaks), and consistency factor (penalize extreme pace variability that indicates unsafe shortcutting); (2) Team metrics: if the team unit earns the incentive rather than the individual, team members can monitor each other for safety compliance; (3) Leading indicators: track near-misses and ergonomic risk behaviors (not just injury outcomes after they occur)
Performance metrics in warehouses have no effect on worker behavior
Injury prevention and productivity metrics are always in direct conflict
explanation: Goodhart's Law: 'When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.' Workers optimize for the specific metric being tracked, not for the underlying goal the metric was intended to measure. UPH optimizes for speed — exactly what is measured — while creating injury risk that is not measured in the performance system. The Amazon UPS parallel: UPS drivers historically had their routes and stop completion rates tracked; drivers who skipped scheduled breaks to 'make route' were rewarded by the metric; this contributed to safety problems. The comprehensive performance framework: Toyota's production system includes quality, safety, and efficiency metrics simultaneously — a worker who improves quality (reducing defect rate) at the cost of slightly lower throughput is still celebrated as improving the system. The key principle: single-metric optimization creates dysfunction; only a balanced scorecard of metrics can capture the full performance picture. The OSHA injury rate benchmark: industry average for warehousing is ~3-4 injuries per 100 workers annually; Amazon's rate has been reported at 6-7 per 100 — more than double. Each injury: workers' compensation cost, reduced productivity during recovery, training replacement worker, potential regulatory fine.

question: A hospital's Emergency Department measures: (1) Average Length of Stay (ALOS = 4.2 hours); (2) Left Without Being Seen (LWBS = 8% of arrivals leave before being treated); (3) Door-to-Doctor Time (average 45 minutes). The hospital board says patient satisfaction is their priority. The ED medical director says these three metrics are the right operational measures. Are they measuring the right things for their stated goal?
options: Operational metrics and patient satisfaction metrics always measure the same thing

Operational metrics correlate with patient satisfaction but measure throughput, not experience: the correlation is real: shorter ALOS → faster service → higher satisfaction; lower LWBS → patients aren't frustrated enough to leave → higher satisfaction; faster door-to-doctor → patients feel attended to quickly → higher satisfaction. The gap: these metrics measure operational efficiency from the system's perspective, not patient experience from the patient's perspective. What they miss: (1) Communication quality: was the patient informed about wait times? Was the diagnosis explained clearly? Did they understand discharge instructions? High ALOS with excellent communication may satisfy patients more than fast ALOS with poor communication; (2) Pain management timeliness: the patient experiencing severe pain during the 45-minute door-to-doctor wait has a very different experience than a patient with a minor complaint; pain assessment and initial intervention before physician visit is a satisfaction driver not captured in these metrics; (3) Discharge experience: a patient who is processed quickly but discharged without understanding their follow-up care has poor real satisfaction despite good operational metrics; (4) The actual satisfaction measure: Press Ganey (the standard hospital patient satisfaction survey) asks about: staff courtesy, clarity of explanations, pain management, cleanliness — not primarily about wait time; operational metrics correlate with but don't directly measure what Press Ganey measures. The better metric portfolio: add door-to-pain-assessment time, HCAHPS (patient satisfaction scores), discharge instruction comprehension score
ED operational metrics are completely unrelated to patient satisfaction
Left Without Being Seen (LWBS) is not a meaningful metric for emergency department performance
explanation: HCAHPS (Hospital Consumer Assessment of Healthcare Providers and Systems): the federal standard for patient satisfaction measurement in US hospitals. HCAHPS results are publicly reported (at CMS's Hospital Compare website) and affect Medicare reimbursement rates — creating financial incentives tied to patient experience, not just operational efficiency. The satisfaction-operational correlation research: Baldrige Award winning hospitals consistently show that improving operational metrics (ALOS, LWBS, door-to-doc) correlates with HCAHPS improvement, but the relationship is not 1:1. Hospitals that improve operations while failing to improve communication and clinical explanation don't capture the full satisfaction uplift. The Cleveland Clinic model: Cleveland Clinic's 'Patients First' transformation (2008-2015) involved adding HCAHPS as an executive compensation metric — aligning C-suite incentives with patient experience. The transformation included operational improvements AND communication training, recognition that the two dimensions together drove HCAHPS improvement more than either alone.

question: A manufacturing company tracks 'Cost Per Unit' (CPU) and has been reducing it by 2% per year through lean manufacturing initiatives. A consultant points out: 'You're optimizing the wrong metric — your On-Time Delivery is 87% and defect rate is 3%. Customers are complaining about quality and delivery, not cost.' The plant manager responds: 'We can't afford to sacrifice efficiency.' How should the company balance these competing operational priorities?
options: Cost per unit is always the primary operational metric for manufacturing companies

Balancing cost, quality, and delivery requires measuring all three and understanding their interdependencies: the false trade-off assumption: the plant manager assumes improving quality or delivery requires increasing cost; the lean manufacturing evidence challenges this: (1) The Cost-of-Quality concept: the cost of poor quality includes: scrap and rework (internal failure cost), warranty claims and returns (external failure cost), customer complaint handling, and lost future sales; if the defect rate is 3% and each defect costs $500 in rework/return, on 100,000 units/year = $1.5M in quality cost; improving defect rate to 0.3% through process improvement (not cost increase) saves $1.35M — more than offsetting the investment in quality improvement; (2) On-time delivery improvement: 87% OTD means 13% of deliveries are late; late deliveries generate expedite costs (premium freight), customer penalties, and customer relationship damage; improving OTD through better scheduling and capacity planning typically reduces cost (less expediting) rather than increasing it; (3) The lean evidence: Toyota's simultaneous achievement of higher quality, better delivery, and lower cost than Western competitors (documented in the 1980s-1990s) disproved the quality-cost trade-off assumption; (4) The metric prioritization: given that customers are complaining about quality and delivery, the appropriate priority is OTD and quality improvement — while monitoring that CPU doesn't dramatically worsen
All manufacturing companies should prioritize delivery over cost at all times
Quality improvements always require significant capital investment in new equipment
explanation: The Quality-Cost-Delivery triangle: traditional operations management taught that you could optimize only two of three (Cost, Quality, Delivery). Toyota's Production System proved this was a false constraint for most businesses — the waste in most manufacturing systems is large enough that improving quality and delivery simultaneously reduces cost. The cost-of-quality framework: Joseph Juran (1950s) established that poor quality is expensive — failures found externally (customer complaints, returns) cost 10× more than failures found internally (inspection before shipment), which cost 10× more than failures prevented (process control that prevents defects from occurring). The investment in quality prevention has a much higher ROI than tolerating defects and managing their downstream consequences. The specific metrics: ISO 9001 certification requires companies to track quality costs systematically — prevention costs, appraisal costs, internal failure costs, and external failure costs. Companies that implement ISO tracking consistently discover that quality is self-financing: the reduction in failure costs more than covers the investment in prevention.

question: A software company's engineering team is measured on: (1) Sprint velocity (story points completed per sprint); (2) Bug count (number of bugs reported after release); (3) Deployment frequency (how often new code is released). These are DORA (DevOps Research and Assessment) metrics. A new VP of Product wants to add 'customer value delivered per sprint' as a fourth metric. The engineering team says this is unmeasurable. Is the VP right or wrong?
options: Customer value metrics are always impossible to quantify for software development teams

The VP is correct that customer value should be measured; the engineering team is right that it's harder — but the difficulty doesn't justify avoiding it: the DORA metrics gap: velocity, bug count, and deployment frequency measure how efficiently engineering operates (productivity, quality, speed); they don't measure whether the work being produced generates business outcomes. The customer value measurement options: (1) Feature adoption rate: within 30 days of deploying a new feature, what % of eligible users use it at least once? A feature with <5% adoption may indicate it wasn't aligned with customer needs; (2) User retention impact: did the release correlate with improved 7-day/30-day retention? (measurable by comparing cohorts before/after); (3) Customer satisfaction change: NPS surveys or in-product CSAT ratings correlate releases to satisfaction; (4) Revenue impact: for revenue-critical features (checkout flow, payment success rate), direct revenue attribution is possible; (5) Support ticket volume change: a release that reduces support contacts about a specific issue delivered measurable value; the measurement challenge: not every feature has direct measurable impact in 30 days; foundational technical work (refactoring, infrastructure) creates future value not captured in feature adoption. The portfolio approach: measure outcome metrics at the portfolio level (are we improving retention, NPS, and revenue?) while tracking output metrics at the sprint level (velocity, bugs)
Engineering productivity metrics are always sufficient to evaluate software team performance
All software features are equally important and should be measured identically
explanation: DORA metrics and engineering outcomes: the DORA (DevOps Research and Assessment) research program at Google (now part of DORA Institute) identified four key software delivery metrics: Deployment Frequency, Lead Time for Changes, Change Failure Rate, and Mean Time to Recovery. These are output metrics (how good is the engineering process?) not outcome metrics (what business results are generated?). The product-engineering metrics gap: engineering teams optimizing for DORA metrics may ship frequently and with low failure rates while shipping features nobody uses. The shift to outcome metrics: Spotify's 'HEART' framework (Happiness, Engagement, Adoption, Retention, Task Success) is an example of outcome metrics that product teams use alongside DORA engineering metrics. The measurement challenge the VP is facing: outcome measurement requires instrumentation (feature usage tracking, A/B test infrastructure), analytical capability (can the team connect releases to outcomes?), and time horizon patience (some features have delayed impact). Amazon's 'Working Backwards' approach partially addresses this: starting with the customer experience story (press release and FAQ) before writing code pre-commits to a customer value hypothesis that can be measured after shipping.

question: A global logistics company tracks 'Cost Per Shipment' (CPS) and has been reducing it through route optimization and hub consolidation. A competitor (Amazon Logistics) enters the market with dramatically lower CPS through advanced routing algorithms and dense urban delivery networks. The company's CPS is now higher than the competitor's. What does this competitive situation reveal about operations metrics and competitive strategy?
options: The company should simply replicate Amazon's technology to match their cost structure

Absolute cost performance means nothing without competitive context; the relevant metric is relative cost position: the company's false comfort: reducing CPS by 2% annually looked good internally; but if competitors reduced CPS by 8% annually, the absolute improvement was actually a competitive decline. The comparative metric: cost advantage vs. specific competitors is more actionable than absolute CPS improvement; tracking 'our CPS vs. Amazon's CPS' versus 'our CPS vs. last year's CPS' reveals the competitive reality. Amazon Logistics' cost structure advantage: (1) Routing density: Amazon delivers 50M+ packages/day in the US; route density (packages per driver-hour in a geographic area) is 3× traditional carriers; dense delivery routes dramatically reduce CPS; (2) Technology investment: algorithms that Amazon spent $500M developing are not easily replicable; (3) Integration: Amazon Logistics integrated with the fulfillment center (packages go directly from FC to van); last-mile integration eliminates handoff cost that traditional carriers incur; (4) Scale: Amazon's volume provides capital to invest in routing technology, electric vehicles, and delivery infrastructure; the strategic response options: (1) Partner with Amazon (Amazon Logistics sometimes subcontracts to local carriers); (2) Differentiate on service quality (e.g., white-glove delivery for high-value items where cost is less important than care); (3) Specialize in segments Amazon doesn't serve well (rural delivery, heavy freight, hazardous materials); (4) Build density in specific markets (become the dominant carrier in one city rather than competing nationally)
Cost per shipment metrics are only relevant for companies with their own delivery fleets
Companies with higher costs than competitors should always compete on price
explanation: Amazon's last-mile logistics disruption: Amazon Logistics (AMZL) now delivers 60%+ of Amazon's own packages — previously a volume that went to UPS and FedEx. The competitive effect on traditional carriers: UPS and FedEx lost significant Amazon volume between 2015 and 2023, forcing route restructuring. The density economics: logistics cost is primarily driven by the number of stops per route and the distance between stops. Amazon's e-commerce density (multiple packages per address in urban areas, known delivery patterns from Prime) creates route efficiency that a general-purpose carrier can't match. The 'operational excellence as commodity' problem: when operational excellence (cost efficiency through lean operations) was a differentiator, being 10% cheaper was a competitive advantage. When competitors achieve equivalent or better operational efficiency through technology (Amazon) or scale (Walmart logistics), the basis of competition shifts. Traditional carriers must find dimensions of competitive advantage that Amazon's algorithm can't replicate: relationship quality for enterprise shippers, specialized handling capabilities, rural network coverage. The metrics lesson: measuring your own performance improvement (CPS down 2%) without measuring competitive position change is like running a race while only watching your own feet — you might be improving while losing.
